preFix.title: Wet-felted scarf with silk-stripe inlay
preFix.subtitle: A 160 × 20 cm merino scarf with bands of tussah silk laid in at the surface
preFix.excerpt: A flat wet-felted scarf in 100% merino roving with tussah silk hankies teased into stripe inlays at the surface. The silk bonds to the wool during fulling, producing lustrous bands across the finished scarf. Pre-felt dimensions allow 40% length shrinkage and 30% width shrinkage.
preFix.sourceNotes: Merino-silk inlay scarves are a standard intermediate wet-felting project taught in UK fibre arts studios. Technique synthesised from general nuno and flat felting practice; not drawn from a specific PD source.

content.text: This scarf uses a merino
roving
base with stripes of
tussah silk
applied as an
inlay
. The silk is teased from
silk hankies
into loose stripes and laid across the width of the scarf before wetting. During
fulling

, the wool fibres migrate through the silk and lock it at the surface, producing a lustrous band that catches light differently from the matte wool background. The finished scarf is 160 cm long and 20 cm wide. Pre-felt dimensions are 267 cm × 29 cm, allowing for 40% length shrinkage and 30% width shrinkage. The intermediate rating reflects the large working area and the need to handle silk carefully to avoid it clumping.

items.name: Tussah silk hankies in contrasting or complementary colours

content.text: Method
Step 1: Prepare the working area

Lay the bamboo rolling mat on a long table and place the felting net on top of it. The net should extend 270 cm along the table (or work in two sections if space is limited). Mark the pre-felt area: 267 cm × 29 cm. This is larger than the table in most kitchens; a long hallway floor works well.
Step 2: Lay the base roving in cross-hatch layers

Pull thin wisps of merino roving and lay the first layer across the 29 cm width, running all wisps along the length of the scarf. Keep layers thin and even. Add a second layer running across the width (short direction). Two
cross-hatch layers
is sufficient for the base; three is acceptable if you want a denser result, but each extra layer adds substantial weight to the finished scarf.
Step 3: Apply the silk stripe inlay

Separate individual layers from the tussah silk hankies. Each hanky peels apart into translucent sheets. Tear or stretch a sheet into a wispy stripe roughly 4-5 cm wide and place it across the full width of the scarf. Space the silk stripes 15-20 cm apart along the length. Keep each stripe sparse: heavy clumps of silk will form thick ridges that are harder to felt through. Aim for a gauzy, semi-transparent band.
Step 4: Wet down through the net

Place the felting net over the entire surface. Using a jug, pour warm soapy water (40°C) slowly and evenly over the net until the roving and silk are saturated. Press down firmly through the net with flat hands to push out air and make the fibres lie flat. Do not rub yet - simply press.
Step 5: Rub through the net to pre-felt

Still working through the net, begin rubbing with a flat hand in small circles. Cover the entire surface, paying attention to the edges. Continue for 10-12 minutes, adding water if the surface begins to feel sticky or dry. Lift the net occasionally and check that the silk stripe is beginning to bed into the wool surface rather than sitting on top as a loose mass. If the silk is loose, press the net back down and rub more firmly over those areas.
Step 6: Roll in sections

Remove the net. Roll the scarf lengthwise inside the bamboo mat, adding a layer of bubble wrap inside to prevent sections sticking together. Roll back and forth firmly for 10 minutes. Unroll, turn 90 degrees, and roll for another 10 minutes. Repeat until the scarf holds together when lifted at one end without stretching noticeably. The scarf will still be larger than the finished dimensions at this stage.
Step 7: Full to finished dimensions

Throw the scarf onto the felting mat 20 times, folding it in thirds between throws to work the whole length. Rub sections vigorously between both palms, working along the length. Measure periodically: the goal is 160 cm long and 20 cm wide. Continue fulling until the scarf reaches those dimensions, or within 5% of them. The silk stripes should now be firmly embedded at the surface with a visible sheen.
Step 8: Rinse and dry

Rinse thoroughly in water at the same temperature as the wash water (30-40°C). Squeeze out excess water gently. Stretch the scarf lengthwise while damp to align the fibres and even out the width. Pin to a foam mat or lay flat to dry. Once dry, the silk stripes will appear more defined than when wet.

items.fix: In future, use no more than a single peeled hanky layer per stripe. On the current scarf, those ridges can be thinned by rubbing them firmly from both sides once the scarf is at pre-felt stage.
items.cause: Too much silk was applied in each stripe, or the silk was not teased into thin enough layers before application
items.problem: The silk stripes have clumped into thick ridges instead of flat bands

items.fix: For the next attempt, use only two base roving layers and place the silk on the very top. If the stripe is invisible on the current piece, it may have migrated through to the underside: check both faces.
items.cause: The silk was too sparse or was buried under too many roving layers
items.problem: The silk has disappeared and is not visible on the surface

items.fix: Check dimensions every 10 minutes during fulling and stop when 160 cm is reached. Once overfulled, the length cannot be restored.
items.cause: Fulling was continued past the intended shrinkage rate
items.problem: The scarf is much shorter than 160 cm after fulling

items.fix: During laying, pay extra attention to the long edges: add an extra wisp of roving along each edge before wetting. While fulling, regularly stroke the edges inward with flat hands.
items.cause: Roving was laid unevenly at the edges, leaving thin spots that shrink faster than the centre
items.problem: The edges are wavy and uneven

content.text: Care and finishing

Hand-wash in cool water with a wool-safe detergent. Do not wring; press and squeeze gently. Lay flat to dry, reshaping while damp if the edges have shifted. The silk stripes are permanently bonded and will not separate during washing. Do not tumble dry.
